turns.title: cli session: stream refactor
turns.prompt: Refactor the parser to stream instead of buffering the whole file.
turns.reply: Done — `parse()` is a generator yielding records line-by-line, so memory stays flat on large inputs. Tests pass.

turns.title: cli session: stream refactor
turns.reply: Added `--limit N`; it short-circuits the generator after N records. Help text updated.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: I keep starting notebooks and abandoning them. What actually makes a notebook stick?
turns.thinking: The user is asking a real personal-systems question, not a product rec. The honest answer is about friction and retrieval, not which app. I should resist listing apps and instead name the two failure modes: capture friction (too slow to write) and retrieval failure (can't find it later). Give a crisp principle.
turns.reply: Two things kill notebooks, and neither is the app. First, capture friction: if writing a thought costs more than thinking it, you won't. Second, retrieval failure: if you can't find a note when it's relevant, the notebook is a write-only grave. A notebook sticks when capture is near-free and past-you reliably hands present-you the right page. Everything else — tags, formatting, the perfect tool — is downstream of those two.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: so how do you make retrieval reliable without spending all day organizing?
turns.thinking: Don't say 'just use search' — that's the lazy answer and it fails for half-remembered notes. The real move is leaving retrieval cues at write-time: a few words future-you would actually search for, in future-you's vocabulary. Organization is expensive; cueing is cheap and happens once.
turns.reply: Stop organizing and start cueing. Organizing is sorting things into folders you'll second-guess; cueing is leaving, at write time, the two or three words future-you will actually reach for. The trick is to write the cue in the vocabulary of the situation where you'll need it, not the situation where you wrote it. A note about a bug isn't filed under 'bugs' — it's cued with the error message you'll paste into search at 2am. Cue once, retrieve forever; the sorting never happens.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: that's basically what transcrub does for conversations, huh
turns.thinking: Nice — the user connects it back to the tool. I can affirm the parallel honestly without overselling: the marks ARE retrieval cues, and the chronological feed is the 'past-you hands present-you the page' part.
turns.reply: Exactly the same shape. The margin marks are cues you (or the model) drop at read-time — a sentence future-you will recognize. The chronological feed is past-you handing present-you the surrounding context instead of a naked snippet. And surfacing the thinking blocks is the part most tools throw away: the reasoning is usually the reusable cue, not the conclusion.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: ok sold. what should I NOT do
turns.reply: Don't migrate. The urge to move everything into the new clean system is the abandonment in disguise — it front-loads all the cost and produces nothing you can use today. Start cueing the next note. Let the old stuff stay where it is; pull it forward only when it's actually relevant. A notebook that demands a migration tax to start is one you'll quit by Thursday.

turns.title: community: alex
turns.reply: Happy to. One sentence on what it does, plus the feeling you want the name to carry, and I'll riff a dozen directions — then we cut.
